Facts.text: Daitokuji temple, Rinzai line, established in Kyoto by Daito Kokushi
Biblioteca Ambrosiana in Milan opens its reading room, second public library in Europe
Mexican border town Ciudad Juárez is founded by Fray García de San Francisco.
Battle of Brihuega in the War of the Spanish Succession: British General James Stanhope captured by French & Spanish forces
1st cremation in US: Henry Laurens
1st issue of Herald of Rutland, VT published
Gustav Freytag's comedy play "The Journalists" premieres in Breslau
Pope Pius IX proclaims Immaculate Conception, makes Mary, free of Original Sin
2,500 reported killed as result of fire at Jesuit Church of La Compana, Santiago, Chile
Pope Pius IX publishes encyclical Quanta cura ("Syllabus errorum")
20th Roman Catholic ecumenical council, Vatican I, opens in Rome
Timothy Eaton founds T. Eaton Co. Limited in Toronto, Canada.
Suriname begins compulsory education for 7-12 years
5,000 armed Boers gather in Paardekraal, South Africa
Vienna's Ring Theater destroyed by gaslight fire, killing an estimated 384-1000 people
Gustaf V of Sweden becomes King of Sweden (1907-50)
Bird banding society found
Construction starts on Palace of Fine Arts in San Francisco
Battle of the Falkland Island: British Royal Navy destroys a German battle squadron
Boers rebelling against the British in South Africa suffer several defeats, with one of their leaders, General Beyers, accidentally drowning
German-US friendship treaty signed
Labour/Liberals win British parliament
Salary & price freeze in Germany
Broadway Theater opens at 1681 Broadway NYC
Coaxial cable patented
French nun Bernadette Soubirous, who saw the vision of the Virgin Mary at Lourdes, is canonized by the Catholic Church
Friedrich Wolf's "Professor Mamlock" premieres in Zurich

The Japanese military police launches a violent suppression of the religious sect Oomoto, beginning with a crackdown on the sect's operational bases of Ayabe and Kameoka in Kyoto Prefecture and the arrest of its leader Onisaburo Deguchi.
Anastasio Somoza García elected President of Nicaragua
NAACP files suit to equalize salaries of black & white teachers
Highest temperature for December in US recorded in La Mesa Calif
Chełmno extermination camp opens, 50 kilometres from Łódź, Poland
London: Dutch government-in-exile declares war on Japan
Russian 16th army recaptures Krijukovo
San Francisco 1st blackout, at 6:15 PM
US and Britain declare war on Japan, US enters World War II
8th Heisman Trophy Award: Frank Sinkwich, Georgia (HB)
US Army rocket plane XS-1 makes 1st powered flight
14th Heisman Trophy Award for US college football awarded to halfback Doak Walker of SMU
Jordan annexs Arabic Palestine
Chinese Nationalist government moves from Chinese mainland to Formosa
AL alters its restrictions on night games, adopting NL's suspended game rule & lifting its ban on lights for Sunday games
1st TV acknowledgement of pregnancy (I Love Lucy)
French troops shoot at anti-colonial demonstrators in Casablanca, Morocco, killing 50
Isaak Ben-Zwi elected President of Israel
19th Heisman Trophy Award: Johnny Lattner, Notre Dame (HB)
WPTZ TV channel 5 in Plattsburgh, NY (NBC) begins broadcasting
21st Heisman Trophy Award: Howard Cassady, Ohio State (HB)
Turkish government of Menderes forms
1st test firing of Vanguard satellite program, TV-0
Guy Mitchell's "Singing the Blues" single goes #1 for 10 weeks

XVI Summer Olympic Games close at the MCG in Melbourne, Australia; start of an Olympic tradition - amidst international tensions, athletes mingle together, parade into and around MCG arena for final appearance to close the Games
Baseball's new Continental League awards its 6th franchise to Atlanta, Georgia
Dom Mintoff demands independence for Malta
MLB expansion team Los Angeles Angels sign a 4 year lease to use Dodger Stadium
Antwerp Belgium diocese forms
Construction workers top off the Space Needle in Seattle, Washington, less 8 months after work began
Larry Costello scores 32 consecutive pts without a miss (NBA rec)
South Africa v NZ, Durban debuts for Eddie Barlow & Peter Pollock
114-day newspaper strike begins in NYC
Attempted coup in British-controlled Brunei
Frank Sinatra, Jr is kidnapped at Harrah's Lake Tahoe (Nevada)
Pan Am Flight 214 crashes outside Elkton, Maryland with 81 killed
Abe Burrows' play "Cactus Flower" premieres in NYC
Nikolai Podgorny succeeds Mikojan as Chairman of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet
US and USSR sign treaty to prohibit nuclear weapons in outer space
NHL California Seals change name to Oakland Seals
The Beatles' "Magical Mystery Tour" EP is released in UK; issued in US as an album, including additional singles from 1967
Greek DC-6B crashes in storm at Athens, 93 killed
Police surprise attack on Black-Panthers in LA
Head of the Catholic Church in Ireland Cardinal William Conway publishes a pamphlet on the topic of segregation in education in Northern Ireland
United Airlines crashes at Chicago's Midway Airport killing 45
39th Heisman Trophy Award: John Cappelletti, Penn State (RB)
Greek monarchy rejected by referendum
Irish Republican Socialist Party forms
Soyuz 16 returns to Earth
Radio station 4ZZZ begins transmitting in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia at 105.7 FM; frequency changes to 102.1 FM in 1978
Asylum Records releases The Eagles fifth studio album "Hotel California"; it spawns two #1 singles and sells over 30 million copies
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
43rd Heisman Trophy Award: Earl Campbell, Texas (RB)
Commencement of the 1st day/night World Series Cricket supertest at VFL Park, Melbourne
"Bravo" network premieres on cable TV
France performs nuclear test
Norman Mayer holds Washington Monument hostage, demanding an end to nuclear weapons. Is killed by police after 10 hrs (he had no explosives)
Suriname army leader Desi Bouterse orders the murders 15 opponents to his military regime
9th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Columbia 6 lands at Edwards AFB
Richard Baker, Zen teacher, steps down from abbotship of San Francisco Zen Center
Europe & 64 developing countries sign Lome III treaty
Ken O'Brien's 96 yard TD pass (NY Jet record) to Wesley Walker
NBC premiere of thriller "Final Jeopardy", starring Richard Thomas and Mary Crosby
NBC premiere of miniseries "Anastasia: The Mystery of Anna" (Part 2)
US House of Representative Democrats select majority leader Jim Wright as 48th Speaker of the House
Occupied Palestinians start "intefadeh" (uprising) against Israel
Peruvian Navy Fokker F27-400M chartered by Alianza Lima FC crashes into Pacific Ocean 7 miles from Jorge Chávez International Airport near Callao; 43 killed, only pilot survives
Knick's set NBA record of 11 3-pointers & sink Bucks, 113-109
Great Britain performs nuclear test
Indians agree to a lease new ballpark in Gateway (Jacobs Field)

NASA discovers life on Earth - in famous control experiment looking for alien life, using spacecraft Galileo's gravity assist fly-by Earth at close altitude of 597 miles (960 km) on its way to Jupiter
Russia, Belorussia & Ukraine form Commonwealth of Ind States
The Romanian Constitution is adopted in a referendum.
NBC announces that "Cheers" will go off the air in May 1993
Spacecraft Galileo makes its second and closest flyby of Earth at a minimum distance of 305 km for a gravity assist on its way to Jupiter
30 killed at religious rebellion in Algeria
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 3734.53
Storm hits western Europe, 11 killed in England
Fire in cinema in Karamay China, 310 killed
Tadjena massacre: 81 people are killed by armed groups in Algeria.

The Caribbean Community Heads of Government meet with the Government of Cuba and declare the date to be "CARICOM-Cuba Day" - To celebrate diplomatic ties between the Caribbean Community (CARICOM) and Cuba.
Cuzco Declaration is signed in Cuzco, Peru, establishing the Union of South American Nations
Ante Gotovina, Croatian army general accused of war crimes, is captured in the Playa de las Américas, Tenerife by the Spanish police.
73rd Heisman Trophy Award: Tim Tebow, Florida (QB)
Kirsty Williams elected as Leader of the Welsh Liberal Democrats. The first female leader of a political party in Wales.
Bombings in Baghdad, Iraq kill 127 and injure 448.
The NBA and players union reach financial agreement to end a 161-day lockout, shortening the season by 16 games
UN climate conference agrees to extend the Kyoto Protocol to 2020
84th Heisman Trophy Award: Kyler Murray, Oklahoma (QB)
Fire in an illegal bag factory in Delhi, India, kills 43
Polar explorers Mike Horn and Børge Ousland complete an 1,800 km (1,118 miles) journey on drifting ice in darkness in the Arctic
Sanna Marin becomes Finland's youngest-ever Prime Minister at the age of 34
Vienna State Opera stages its first show by a female composer in 150 years - Olga Neuwirth’s opera, “Orlando”
Former Israeli space security chief Haim Eshed says Aliens and a Galactic Federation exist andDonald Trumpknows about it, in article published in the Jerusalem Post
The UK begins vaccinating for COVID-19 using the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine
American basketball star Brittany Griner is released by Russian authorities in a prisoner exchange for Russian arms dealer Viktor Bout; Griner detained on drug smuggling charges since February 2022
Iran announces first known execution of a protester from recent anti-government unrest, after convicting a man in a revolutionary court of "moharebeh" (enmity against God)
UN investigation in DR Congo finds 131 people were massacred in November by M23 rebel group in Kishishe and Bambo villages, in east of the country
Exhibition "The Art of Zen" featuring ink painting "Persimmons" the 'Zen Mona Lisa' by 13th century Chinese monk Muqi opens In San Francisco
Classic Baseball Era Committee of National Baseball Hall elects Philadelphia Phillies/Chicago White Sox Dick Allen and Pittsburgh Pirates/Oakland Athletics Dave Parker for inclusion
American Revolutionary War:George Washington's retreating army crosses the Delaware River from New Jersey to Pennsylvania
CaptainJames Cookleaves the Society Islands
Ludwig van Beethoven's 7th Symphony in A, premieres in Vienna withBeethovenconducting
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Luisa Miller" premieres in Naples
Dion Boucicault's stage drama "The Poor of New York" opens at Wallack's Lyceum Theatre, NYC
Abraham Lincolnissues his Amnesty Proclamation and plan for Reconstruction of the South
James Clerk Maxwell's paper "A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field" is 1st read by the Royal Society in London (published by the Royal Society 1865)
The Clifton Suspension Bridge, designed byIsambard Kingdom Brunel, is finally opened in Bristol, England, 5 years after his death
James-Younger gang led byJesse Jamesrobs a train at Muncie, Kansas stealing $30,000
Aleksandr Ostrovsky's "Volki i Ovsty" premieres in St Petersburg
American Federation of Labor (AFL) formed by 26 craft unionsSamuel Gomperselected AFL president
Battle at Amba Alagi: Ethiopian EmperorMenelik IIdrives Italian General Baratieri out
Oliver Wendell Holmes Jrbecame Associate Justice on US Supreme Court
Wilhelm IIof Germany calls 'War Council'
Irving Berlin's musical "Watch your Step" premieres in NYC
Jean Sibelius' 5th Symphony in E premieres
John McCrae's poem "In Flanders Fields" appears anonymously in "Punch" magazine
John Van Druten's comedic play "Voice of the Turtle" premieres in NYC
"On the Town", the film adaptation of the Broadway musical, starringGene Kelly,Frank Sinatra, Ann Miller andVera-Ellen, premieres
Dwight D. Eisenhowergives his "Atoms for Peace" speech at the United Nations in New York
Maxwell Anderson's play "Bad Seed", based on the book by William March, premieres in NYC
Black Ealing comedy "The Ladykillers", directed by Alexander Mackendrick and starringAlec Guinnessis released in the UK
Brooklyn catcherRoy Campanellawins his 3rd MVP Award

CBS broadcasts "The Sound of Jazz", live from NYC: an all-star program featuring swing era and contemporary jazz artists, includingCount Basie, Red Allen, Ben Webster;, Thelonious Monk, and Gerry Mulligan; highlight is the final collaboration ofBillie Holidayand Lester Young on "Fine and Mellow"
Musical "Peter Pan", starringMary Martinbroadcast, for a 3rd time, on NBC as a special event, videotaped in color for future rebroadcasts
Philadelphia Warriors centerWilt Chamberlainscores 78 points in 151-147 3OT defeat to LA Lakers at Philadelphia Civic Center; 2nd highest game total in the NBA history
Funeral forQueen Wilhelminaof Holland (New Kerk, Delft)
PopePaul VIcloses final session of the Second Vatican Council - an influential ecumenical council that significantly modernized church practices
New York Yankee trade, outfielderRoger Maristo St' Louis Cardinals for Charlie Smith
Romanian tennis starIlie Năstasewins his 3rd consecutive season-ending ATP Masters Grand Prix title with a 6–3, 7–5, 4–6, 6–3 win over Dutchman Tom Okker in Boston, Massachusetts
UN General Assembly re-electsKurt WaldheimSecretary-General
"The Deer Hunter", directed by Michael Cimino and starringRobert De Niro,Christopher WalkenandMeryl Streep, premieres in Los Angeles (Academy Awards Best Picture 1979)
Annie Leibovitzhas a photo-shoot withJohn Lennon, the last person to professionally photograph him before he is murdered on the same day
"Sophie's Choice", directed by Alan J. Pakula and based on William Styton's 1979 novel of the same name, starringMeryl StreepandKevin Kline, is released (Academy Awards Best Actress 1983)
Colombian writerGabriel García Márquezreceives the Nobel Prize for Literature
Ringo Starrhosts "Saturday Night Live";Herbie Hancockis the musical guest
Flyers'Ron Hextallbecomes 1st NHL goalie to actually score a goal, shooting the puck into Boston Bruins empty net in 5-2 win at The Spectrum in Philadelphia
US PresidentRonald Reaganand Soviet leaderMikhail Gorbachevsign a treaty eliminating medium range nuclear missiles
Darryl Strawberryindicted on tax evasion charges
Krzysztof Pendereckiconducts the Munich Philharmonic in premiere of his Symphony No. 3
With the second launch of theSpaceXDragon, SpaceX becomes the first privately held company to successfully launch, orbit and recover a spacecraft.
Brian Ortegafights Max Holloway at UFC 231 and loses by doctor stoppage, earning Fight of the Night
US PresidentDonald Trumpannounces John Kelly will be stepping down as White House Chief of Staff at the end of the year
Olaf Scholzis sworn as the new Chancellor of Germany, replacingAngela Merkelafter 16 years
Canadian pop singerCeline Dionreveals via Instagram that she has been diagnosed with Stiff Person Syndrome (SPS), a rare neurological disorder
Documentary series "Harry & Meghan" aboutPrince Harry, Duke of Sussex andMeghan, Duchess of Sussexbegins streaming on Netflix
Trevor Noahhosts his final episode of late-night talk and satirical news television program "The Daily Show"
Kawhi Leonardscores a season-high 41 points in a win over the Utah Jazz
43,000 Ukrainian soldiers have died in the country's war with Russia, with 370,000 injured, according to PresidentVolodymyr Zelensky

American singer-songwriterTaylor Swiftconcludes "The Eras Tour" with her 149th show, at BC Place in Vancouver, Canada; the 21 month international concert tour is the highest-grossing of all time, with box office gross just over $2 billion

Dutch driverMax Verstappenfinishes 6th in the season-ending Abu Dhabi GP to claim his 4th consecutive World F1 title; Lando Norris wins the race as McLaren takes Constructors C'ship for first time in 26 years

Kennedy Center Honors ceremony held for filmmakerFrancis Ford Coppola, jazz musician Arturo Sandoval, singer-songwriter Bonnie Raitt, rock band The Grateful Dead, and Harlem's Apollo Theater, in Washington D.C.
Syrian PresidentBashar al-Assadresigns and flees to Moscow after rebel forces sweep into the capital city of Damascus
Eamon de Valera publicly repudiates Anglo-Irish Treaty
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltdelivers his famous "Day of Infamy" speech to a joint session of Congress a day after the bombing of Pearl Harbor
Maria Anna of Bavaria, Empress consort of the Holy Roman Empire, born in Munich, Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1616)
Christina, Queen of Sweden who abdicated after becoming Catholic (1644-54), born in Tre Kronor Castle, Stockholm (d. 1689)
Horatio Walpole, 1st Baron Walpole of Wolterton, English diplomat, born in Houghton, Cambridge, England (d. 1757)
Maria Josepha of Austria, Queen consort of Poland (1734-57), born in Hofburg Palace, Vienna, Austria (d. 1757)
Anton de Haen, Austrian medical expert (Ratio Medendi), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1776)
Francis I, Holy Roman Emperor (1745-1765), born in Ducal Palace of Nancy, Lorraine, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1765)
Jan Althuysen, Frisian vicar and poet (Frisianche rymlery), born in Franeker, Netherlands (d. 1763)
Claude-Bénigne Balbastre, French organist, harpsichordist, and composer, born in Dijon, France (d. 1799)
Johann Georg von Zimmermann, Swiss philosophical author and physician, born in Brugg, Aargau, Switzerland (d. 1795)

František Xaver Dušek, Czech pianist, harpsichordist, and composer, born in Chotěborky, Vilantice, Kingdom of Boehmia (d. 1799)

Maximilien Gardel, French ballet dancer and choreographer (Menuet Reine), born in Mannheim, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1787)
Pierre-Joseph Candeille, French composer, born in Estaires, France (d. 1827)
John Fawcett, English sacred music composer, born in Wennington, Lancashire, England (d. 1867)
Jacques-François Gallay, French horn player and composer, born in Perpignan, France (d. 1864)
Peter Andreas Hansen, Danish-German astronomer (Hansen hypothesis), born in Tønder, Schleswig, Denmark (d. 1874)
Louis Schindelmeisser, German clarinetist and composer, born in Königsberg, Prussia (d. 1864)
Adolph Menzel, German painter and graphic artist, born in Breslau, Prussian Silesia (d. 1905)
August Belmont, Prussian-American financier and diplomat (U.S. Minister to the Netherlands 1853-57), born in Alzey, Hesse, Germany (d. 1890)
Christian Emil Krag-Juel-Vind-Frijs, Danish nobleman and politician (Council President of Denmark 1865-70), born in Frijsenborg, Denmark (d. 1896)
Josip Runjanin, Croatian Serb composer (Croatian National Anthem) and soldier, born in Vinkovci (d. 1878)
Luther Prentice Bradley, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1910)
Friedrich Siemens, German industrialist, born in Menzendorf, Germany (d. 1904)
Clinton Bowen Fisk, American abolitionist, Brevet Major General (Union Army), helped establish 1st free public schools in the South, born in York, New York (d. 1890)
Robert Bullock, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Greenville, North Carolina (d. 1905)
Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson, Norwegian author "Pa Guds Veje" and Nobel laureate 1903, born in Kvikne, Norway (d. 1910)
Émile Reynaud, French inventor of the praxinoscope who created the first projected animated films (Pantomimes Lumineuses), born in Montreuil, Seine-Saint-Denis, France (d. 1918)
Aristide Maillol, French painter and sculptor (Seated Woman), born in Banyuls-sur-Mer, Roussillon, France (d. 1944)
George Méliès, French early filmmaker (A trip to the Moon), born in Paris (d. 1938)
William C. Durant, American industry pioneer, founded General Motors, Frigidaire, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1947)
Georges Feydeau, French playwright (La Dame de Chez Maxim's), born in Paris (d. 1921)
Jacques Hadamard, French mathematician (proved the prime number theorem), born in Versailles, France (d. 1963)
Ernst Moro, Austrian physician and pediatrician (Moro reflex), born in Laibach, Duchy of Carniola, Austria-Hungary (d. 1951)
Paul Ladmirault, French composer, born in Nantes, France (d. 1944)
Padraic Colum, Irish novelist and poet (Collected Poems), born in Columcille, County Longford, Ireland (d. 1972)
Manuel Ponce, Mexican composer (Estrellita), born in Fresnillo, Zacatecas, Mexico (d. 1948)
Vicente Emilio Sojo, Venezuelan composer, born in Guatire, Miranda state, Venezuela (d. 1974)
Fiske Kimball, American architect and art historian, born in Newton, Massachusetts (d. 1955)
Paul Cavanagh, British actor (The Woman in Green, Tarzan & his Mate), born in Chislehurst, England (d. 1964)
Hervey Allen, American writer and poet (Anthony Adverse), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1949)
Bohuslav Martinů, Czech, composer (Hry o Marti), born in Polička, Bohemia (d. 1959)
Herbert "Bert" Hinkler, Australian aviator who made first solo flight from England to Australia (1928), born in Bundaberg, Queensland (d. 1933)
E. C. Segar, American cartoonist (Popeye), born in Chester, Illinois (d. 1938)
James Thurber, American humorist, playwright, and cartoonist (Men, Women & Dogs; The Male Animal; The Secret Life of Walter Mitty), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1961)

Leslie Heward, English organist, conductor (City of Birmingham Orchestra, 1930-42; Cape Town Orchestra, 1924-26), and composer (Quodlibet; The Witches' Sabbath), born in Liversedge, Yorkshire, England (d. 1943)
James "Pigmeat" Jarrett, American blues pianist, and singer, born in Cordele, Georgia (d. 1995)[1]

Sarah Williamson, American missionary in Liberia, born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 1986)

Wifredo Lam, Cuban artist (The Jungle), born in Sagua La Grande, Cuba (d. 1982)
Adele Simpson, American fashion designer (Neiman-Marcus Award-1946), born in New York City (d. 1995)

Kitty Muggeridge, British author and translator, born in Chateau d'Oex, Switzerland (d. 1994)
Zoltán Székely, Hungarian violinist and composer, born in Kocs, Hungary (d. 2011)

Ernst Hermann Meyer, German composer, born in Berlin (d. 1988)

Julius Silverman, British Labour Party politician, born in Leeds, England (d. 1996)
(Lloyd) "Slim" Andrews, American comic actor (Buffalo Bill in Tomahawk Territory), and singer-songwriter, born in Gravette, Arkansas (d. 1992)[1]
Leo Shuken, American film music composer and arranger (Stagecoach; The Fabulous Dorseys; The Unsinkable Molly Brown), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1976)
Richard Llewellyn, Welsh novelist (How Green Was My Valley), born in Hendon, Middlesex, England (d. 1983)
Tony Aubin, French composer (Actaeon; Sinfonie Romantique), conductor, and educator (Paris Conservatory, 1944-77), born in Paris (d. 1981)

Cleo Brown, American jazz pianist, born in Meridian, Mississippi (d. 1995)
Lee J. Cobb [Leo Jacoby], American actor (Virginian, 12 Angry Men, On the Waterfront), born in New York City (d. 1976)

George Scheuer, Austrian writer and journalist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1996)

Ian Johnson, Australian cricket spin bowler and captain (45 Tests; 109 wickets), born in North Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1998)

Gérard Souzay, French baritone, born in Angers, France (d. 2004)

McDonald Bailey, British-Trinidadian athlete (Olympic bronze 100m 1952; 100m WR 10.2s 1951-56), born in Williamsville, Trinidad (d. 2013)
Ronald Gulliford, English educationalist (Dean of the Faculty of Education, Birmingham University 1979-81), born in Manchester (d. 1997)
Terence Morgan, British actor (Sir Francis Drake, Curse of the Mummy's Tomb), born in London, England (d. 2005)
Terence Weil, British cellist (Melos Ensemble), born in London (d. 1995)
George Fullerton, South African cricketer (South African keeper-batsman 1947-51), born in Kensington, South Africa (d. 2002)

Ana Ofelia Murguía, Mexican actress (Mamá Coco-Coco; La Reina de la Noche), born in Mexico City (d. 2023)

Johnny Green, American basketball power forward (NBA All-Star 1962, 63, 65, 71; NY Knicks, Baltimore Bullets, SD Rockets, Philadelphia 76ers, Cincinnati Royals/KC-Omaha Kings), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2023)
Dharmendra, Indian actor (Sholay), born in Nasrali, Punjab, British India
Tatiana Zatulovskaya, Israeli chess player (Women's Soviet Chess Championship 1960, 62, 63), born in Baku, Azerbaijan Soviet Socialist Republic, USSR (d. 2017)
Barbara Hulanicki, British fashion design, founder of Biba, born in Warsaw, Poland[1]
Bob Daley [Robert A. Daly], American business executive and CEO of Warner Brothers (1980-99), born in Brooklyn, New York City
Brad Jurjens, Estonian director (The Bank Job), born in Viljandi, Estonia
Buster Farrer, South African cricket batsman (6 Tests, HS 40; Border), born in King William's Town, Cape Province, South Africa (d. 2025)
David Carradine, American actor (Kung Fu, Mean Streets, Kill Bill V.1 & 2), born in Hollywood, California (d. 2009)
Malcolm Forsyth, South African-Canadian trombonist and composer (Atayoskewin), born in Pietermaritzburg, South Africa, (d. 2011)

Arne Næss Jr., Norwegian mountain climber and businessman, born in Germany (d. 2004)
James MacArthur, American actor (Danny Williams-Hawaii 5-0), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2010)
Jan van Houwelingen, Dutch politician and Undersecretary of Defense (CDA), born in Leerdam, Netherlands (d. 2013)
Malcolm Price, Welsh rugby union centre (9 Tests Wales, 5 British & Irish Lions; Pontypool RFC) and rugby league centre (2 Tests GB; Oldham RLFC, Rochdale Hornets), born in Pontypool, Wales (d. 2024)
Nelsinho Rosa, Brazilian soccer coach (Saudi Arabia, Fluminense, Vasco da Gama), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 2023)
James Galway, Irish classical flutist known as "The Man With the Golden Flute", born in Belfast, Northern Ireland
